source_metadata.title_raw: Dennis J. Stryker - A-19-134 - July 25, 2019 - San Diego

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 25, 2019

Dennis J. Stryker
3555 Fifth Ave, Suite 205
San Diego, CA 92103

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-134

Dear Mr. Stryker:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding Section 1090.¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under Section 1090, not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest, including Public Contract Code.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Diego County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

Does Section 1090 prohibit the City of San Diego ("City") from entering into a project where a subconsultant of the prime design firm previously worked as a subconsultant to a different prime engineering firm and provided technical analysis determining the feasibility of certain structures that will ultimately be part of the current project?

CONCLUSION

No, because the provided facts do not indicate that the subconsultant exerted influence over the City's contracting decisions.

___________________________
¹ All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-134
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTER

The City developed a Chollas Creek Watershed Master Plan to identify water quality
improvement projects to help meet the City's goals relating to pollution reduction in storm water
entering Chollas Creek. The City contracted with Terra Tech to help identify the water quality
projects over the entire watershed. The City subsequently contracted with Michael Baker
International ("MBI") for assistance with a technical study to further evaluate the feasibility of a
water quality infrastructure improvement project that had been identified in the Draft Chollas Creek
Watershed Master Plan. The identified water quality project included an "Underground Storage
Vault" with biofiltration soil mix located east of the intersection of Streamview Drive and 54th
Street. Geosyntec worked with MBI as a subconsultant and assisted with the feasibility study under
City staff supervision. One of the City's requested deliverables for the contract was a conceptual
plan and profile drawing with utility conflict checks ("Conceptual Plan"), which was used to
demonstrate that the plan was feasible.

After the feasibility study and Conceptual Plan was completed, the City elected to
incorporate the development of an Underground Storage Vault into a project known as the Design
of Streamview Drive Improvements Phase 2 Contract No. H197062 ("Streamview Drive Project").
The Streamview Drive Project site is located along Streamview Drive from 54th Street to
Lynn/Michael Street and from Gayle Street to College Avenue. The specific purpose of the project
is to provide roadway improvements to reduce excessive speeds and increase pedestrian and
bicyclist mobility on Streamview Drive, and the project involves the replacement of water and
sewer infrastructure along the street corridor. There is existing drainage infrastructure in
Streamview Drive and it is the City's goal to incorporate water quality improvements into the
Streamview Drive Project to reduce pollutant discharge into Chollas Creek. The Conceptual Plan
developed by MBI and Geosyntec was included in the City's Request for Proposal for the design of
the Streamview Drive Project. The City subsequently selected Rick Engineering Company ("Rick")
as the most qualified consultant for the Streamview Drive Project. Rick plans to use Geosyntec as a
subconsultant on the Streamview Drive Project.

You have stated that Geosyntec "provided technical analysis based upon the request of the
City to determine whether a structure and concept provided by the City, and others not part of the
[Streamview Drive] Project, was feasible. Geosyntec had no responsibility, nor any ability, to
influence the City regarding the Project since it was not the prime consultant in the previous project
nor in the leadup studies for the Project."

ANALYSIS

Section 1090 provides, "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, judicial district,
and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made by them in
their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." Section 1090 is
concerned with financial interest, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public
officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of
their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.)

File No. A-19-134
Page No. 3

Interpreting the term "employees," as used in Section 1090, the California Supreme Court
has affirmed the long-standing rule (developed in case law) that independent contractors are not
categorically excluded from Section 1090, stating, "[l]iability under the statute can extend to
independent contractors who have duties to engage in or advise on public contracting." (People v.
Superior Court (Sahlolbei) (2017) 3 Cal.5th 230, 233; see also, id. at p. 240, quoting Stigall, supra,
58 Cal.2d at p. 570 ("[S]ection 1090 liability extends only to independent contractors who can be
said to have been entrusted with 'transact[ing] on behalf of the Government.'").)

However, not all independent contractors are covered by Section 1090. (Sahlolbei, supra, at
p. 240.) "An individual's status as an official under [Section 1090] turns on the extent to which the
person influences an agency's contracting decisions or otherwise acts in a capacity that demands the
public trust." (Ibid., quoting Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton (2010) 186
Cal.App.4th 1114, 1125.) In Hub City, the court held that an independent contractor that exerts
"considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency" is subject to Section
1090. (Hub City, supra, at pp. 1124-1125.)

Previously, we have advised that consultants who play a limited technical role and are
removed from influencing the contracting decisions of a public agency are not subject to Section
1090. (La Salle Advice Letter, No. A-17-074 and Green Advice Letter, No. A-16-084.)

The facts in this case demonstrate that Geosyntec performed work of a limited, technical
nature as a subconsultant with MJH regarding the feasibility of a water quality project that included
the Underground Storage Vault. The Conceptual Plan, which incorporated the feasibility study
performed by Geosync, was included in the RFP and was available to all contractors who bid on the
project. There are no facts indicating that Geosyntec, in its role as a subcontractor on the
Conceptual Plan, influenced the City's contracting decisions, or had the potential to exert
considerable influence on the City's contracting with Rick. Accordingly, the City's contracting with
Rick, using Geosyntec as a subconsultant, for the Streamview Drive Project would not violate
Section 1090.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By:     Kevin Cornwall
        Counsel, Legal Division

KMC:aja

sections.question:

contract where a subconsultant of the prime design firm previously worked as a subconsultant to a 
different prime design engineering firm and provided technical analysis determining the feasibility 
of certain structures that will ultimately be part of the current project?

No, because the provided facts do not indicate that the subconsultant exerted influence over 
the City's contracting decisions.

sections.analysis:
Section 1090 provides, "[ m ]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, judicial district, 
and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made by them in 
their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." Section 1090 is

Superior Court (Sahlolbei) (2017) 3 Cal.5th 230, 233; see also, id. at p. 240, quoting Stigall, supra,

the Underground Storage Vault. The Conceptual Plan, which incorporated the feasibility study

project. There are no facts indicating that Geosyntec, in its role as a subcontractor on the

considerable influence on the City's contracting decisions. Accordingly, the City's contracting with
